Title: Netflix Is Getting Crushed. Here’s Why I’ll Start Buying

Full Content:
Investing Netflix trades near its 52-week low of $65 but carries a $161 price target, implying 138% upside with 90% confidence. Reed Hastings personally bought 794,250 shares at depressed prices while Netflix executed its largest-ever $4.7 billion buyback, with $27 billion still authorized. Sending You to Google News in 3 © hocus-focus / iStock Unreleased via Getty Images Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX | NFLX Price Prediction) trades well below our 24/7 Wall St. price target. Shares closed at $67.68 after falling 27.9% year-to-date and 44.1% over the past year. Our proprietary model points materially higher on a 12-month view, and the drawdown looks like an entry point rather than a warning. Our 24/7 Wall St. price target for Netflix is $161.40, implying triple-digit upside from current levels. This reflects a stock that has become genuinely cheap relative to its earnings power. NFLX is down 8.44% in the past week and 12.64% over the past month, now sitting 23% below the 52-week high of $126.71 and near the 52-week low of $65.08. Q2 2026 delivered EPS of $0.80 on revenue of $12.559 billion, a slight revenue miss against a 13.37% YoY gain. Free cash flow fell 32.73% on higher cash taxes, and the earnings-day narrative was harsh: “Netflix beat earnings, did its biggest buyback and then restricted access to its engagement data and fell 12% through two days.” The market wanted a clean quarter and got a messy one. The bull thesis rests on advertising. Netflix guided FY2026 ad revenue to roughly $3 billion, essentially doubling from $1.5 billion in 2025, with advertiser count up 70% YoY to over 4,000. Full-year 2026 guidance calls for revenue of $51 to $51.4 billion, operating margin of 31.5%, and FCF near $12.5 billion. Netflix repurchased $4.7 billion of stock in Q2, its largest buyback ever, with $27.1 billion still authorized. Founder Reed Hastings bought 794,250 shares in May and June at depressed prices, signaling insider conviction. Our bull case target sits at $173.18. Bears have real ammunition. Content amortization is front-loaded into H1 2026, pressuring near-term margins, and $1 billion in debt matures later in 2026. Prediction markets on Polymarket assign only a 18% probability that NFLX finishes this week above $70, and composite sentiment has fallen 21.79 points over 30 days. Bulls counter that the Q2 FCF drop was driven by cash tax timing and the Warner Bros. termination. Our bear case still lands at $130.55, well above today’s price. Walt Disney (NYSE:DIS) is the natural streaming comp given its Disney+ and Hulu streaming platforms. Disney’s SVOD margins remain well below Netflix’s. That margin gap makes Netflix’s 33.4% operating margin premium-worthy and supports the higher multiple in our target. Spotify (NYSE:SPOT) is the audio streaming comp, continuing to grow Premium Subscribers at a healthy pace. Spotify trades at a much richer multiple than Netflix on forward earnings, making the discount on NFLX look mispriced relative to its scale, margins, and buyback capacity. The peer set makes our 24/7 Wall St. price target look reasonable. The 24/7 Wall St. price target is $161.40, our recommendation is buy, and confidence sits at 90%. The combination of a doubling ad business, a $27 billion buyback runway, and founder-level insider buying into weakness is compelling. Investors may want to watch for a move toward the $65 52-week low as H2 FCF confirms guidance. Key risks include Q3 revenue missing the $12.86 billion guide or the $1 billion refinancing hitting at penal rates. These projections assume Netflix executes on ad-tier scale-up and defends operating margins. Significant upside or downside could come from live sports economics and GenAI content adoption pace. Title: Bitcoin long-term holder supply hits all-time high of 16.64 million BTC
URL: https://cryptobriefing.com/bitcoin-long-term-holder-supply-all-time-high/
Time Published: 2026-07-22T11:51:52Z
Full Content:
Searching... Roughly 83% of all Bitcoin in circulation hasn't moved in at least 155 days, signaling a level of conviction that historically precedes major price runs. Share Bitcoin investors are sitting on their hands, and the data says that’s a very good sign. Long-term holder supply just hit 16.64 million BTC, a new all-time high, according to Coinglass data shared on July 21. That figure represents roughly 83% of Bitcoin’s total circulating supply. In plain English: five out of every six Bitcoin in existence haven’t budged in over five months. The long-term holder (LTH) metric tracks coins that have sat untouched for at least 155 days. It’s a proxy for investor conviction. When LTH supply rises, it means holders are choosing not to sell. When it drops, it means coins are moving to exchanges or new buyers, typically during periods of distribution or panic. Back in November 2025, LTH supply had cratered to 14.33 million BTC during a price correction that dragged Bitcoin toward the $80K range. Roughly 2.3 million BTC shifted from long-term to short-term classification in a matter of weeks. Since then, the trend has completely reversed. Holders who bought during the correction, or who simply refused to sell through it, have now aged their coins past the 155-day threshold. The result is a net addition of over 2.3 million BTC back into the long-term category in about eight months. The previous high-water mark for LTH supply was near 16.4 million BTC, recorded before a consolidation phase that followed the 2024 spot ETF launches. This new reading eclipses that level, suggesting the post-ETF reshuffling of Bitcoin ownership has largely settled into stronger hands. CryptoQuant CEO Ki Young Ju has pointed out that strategic buyers and ETFs have been absorbing Bitcoin from older large holders. Early whales have been taking profits, while institutions, corporate treasuries, and ETF vehicles step in as the new long-term base. When Bitcoin moves from an individual whale’s wallet to a Fidelity or BlackRock ETF custodian, it tends to stay put. These vehicles don’t panic-sell on a 5% dip. Their mandates and structures create a kind of structural demand floor that didn’t exist in prior cycles. When 83% of supply is effectively off the market, the amount available for active trading shrinks dramatically. Less liquid supply means each incremental buyer has outsized impact on price, and each seller has less inventory to dump. Extended periods of LTH accumulation preceded both the 2017 and 2021 bull markets. In 2015, long-term holders quietly stacked coins for months before a rally that eventually took Bitcoin from under $300 to nearly $20,000. A similar accumulation phase in 2019 preceded the run to $69,000. The risk scenario to watch is a sudden reversal. If LTH supply begins declining rapidly, it would indicate that long-term holders are distributing into strength, a classic top signal. -715.06 -191.45 + 261.00 + 2,857.00 + 3,271.00 -715.06 -191.45 -191.45 + 261.00 + 261.00 + 2,857.00 Sensex Today, Nifty 50 | Stock Market Highlights - Find here all the updates related to Sensex, Nifty, BSE, NSE, share prices and Indian stock markets for 22nd July 2026. Indian equity benchmarks posted their biggest single-day decline in two weeks on Wednesday as escalating tensions in the Middle East drove Brent crude above $95 a barrel, raising concerns over inflation and economic growth. The Sensex fell 715.06 points (0.92%) to close at 76,755.05, while the Nifty 50 declined 191.45 points (0.79%) to 23,996.25, marking their third straight session of losses. Broader markets also weakened, with mid-cap and small-cap indices falling 1.1% and 1.5%, respectively. Banking, IT and pharma stocks led the decline, while auto and FMCG emerged as the only resilient sectors, supported by strong earnings from Bajaj Auto, TVS Motor and Nestle India. Investor sentiment remained fragile amid fears that higher oil prices could fuel inflation, widen India’s trade deficit and hurt corporate margins. The rupee weakened 34 paise to close at 96.59 against the US dollar, pressured by rising crude prices, geopolitical uncertainty and weak domestic equities. * Sensex plunged 715.06 points (0.92%) to close at 76,755.05, after falling as much as 828.92 points intraday. * Nifty 50 slipped 191.45 points (0.79%) to end below the 24,000 mark at 23,996.25. * Benchmarks extended losses for a third straight session, recording their biggest single-day fall in two weeks. * Brent crude surged 4.68% to $95.27 a barrel, fuelling concerns over inflation, India’s import bill and corporate margins. * Banking stocks led the sell-off, while IT, cement and select financial stocks also came under pressure. * InterGlobe Aviation, Infosys, SBI, UltraTech Cement, ICICI Bank and Axis Bank were among the top Sensex losers. * Hindustan Unilever, NTPC, Power Grid and Titan bucked the trend to close in the green. * Broader markets remained under pressure, with selling seen across most sectors despite a steady start to the Q1 earnings season. * Investor sentiment weakened amid escalating Middle East tensions, rising oil prices and concerns over potential US tariffs on pharmaceutical imports. * Foreign Institutional Investors (FIIs) remained net buyers in the previous session, purchasing ₹1,650.16 crore worth of equities. The Court held that Vedanta became ineligible for an extension after it unilaterally deducted the Government’s share of profit petroleum to recover the Special Additional Excise Duty, which it was itself liable to pay. Markets remained under significant pressure through Wednesday’s midday session, with the Sensex and Nifty sliding sharply as selling intensified across banking, information technology, and pharmaceutical stocks. The Indian pharmaceutical sector is navigating a sharp divergence between robust domestic fundamentals and emerging global trade risks. While the domestic market accelerates toward its strongest annual performance in five years, news of proposed United States trade tariffs on generic medicines sparked immediate selling pressure across major pharma stocks today, testing investor sentiment. The primary catalyst behind today’s market pullback is a newly proposed US tariff schedule targeting generic drug imports. Under the phased plan, foreign generic medicines will maintain a duty-free window through 2027, followed by a sharp increase to a 100% tariff starting in 2028, with potential escalations to 200% thereafter. This follows earlier policy shifts in April 2026 under Section 232, which imposed 100% tariffs on patented drugs while leaving generics exempt. Because Indian drugmakers export approximately $8 billion to $9 billion worth of products—predominantly generics—to the US annually, the proposal introduces a significant long-term structural risk to export margins. Despite these immediate international headwinds, the domestic foundation of the sector remains remarkably strong. Driven by expanding demand across major therapeutic segments, the Indian Pharmaceutical Market is projected to grow 11.3% in 2026—a notable acceleration from the 8.1% recorded in 2025. Looking further out, the domestic market is on track to expand from $60.3 billion in 2026 to $79.7 billion by 2031, providing a solid growth buffer against foreign policy shifts. From a technical perspective, the sector’s overall financial health reflects this underlying domestic momentum. The Nifty Pharma Index continues to hold a healthy long-term uptrend, trading near the 25,740 to 25,800 range—well above its 50-day moving average of 24,800 and 200-day moving average of 23,100, and within striking distance of its 52-week high of 26,135. Crucially, the two-year grace period before the US generic tariffs take effect offers Indian drugmakers a vital operational runway. this window gives companies sufficient time to realign global supply chains, renegotiate distribution contracts, or establish local manufacturing capabilities directly within the US. Additionally, export diversification is gaining momentum through the newly effective India-UK Free Trade Agreement, which removes import duties on Indian pharmaceuticals entering the UK market and is expected to drive a 8% to 10% boost in UK-bound exports next year. While policy uncertainties surrounding US trade may introduce short-term volatility, the sector’s broader trajectory remains anchored in solid fundamentals. Companies holding strong domestic market share, active raw-material manufacturing bases, and advanced pipelines in novel formulations are best positioned to navigate global supply chain shifts and sustain long-term growth. Trident Limited reported a 10 per cent rise in standalone net profit for the quarter ended June 30, 2026, at ₹152.93 crore compared to ₹139.36 crore in the same quarter last year, as the textile-to-paper conglomerate also announced plans to incorporate a new domestic wholly owned subsidiary focused on overseas brand-building. The trust allotted 11.10 crore units to 59 funds at Rs 152 per unit, according to a circular on BSE. Prazim Trading and Investment Co Pvt Ltd, an entity associated with Premji Invest, emerged as the largest anchor investor with an allocation worth Rs 300 crore. Other prominent investors in the anchor book included ICICI Prudential Life Insurance Company, SBI Life Insurance Company, HDFC Mutual Fund, SBI Mutual Fund, Nippon India Mutual Fund, and Tata Mutual Fund. Overall, 12 mutual funds, through 26 schemes, participated in the anchor book. The trust has fixed a price band at Rs 151-152 per unit for its public issue, which will close on July 24. The issue is a 100 per cent book-built Offer for Sale (OFS) of units aggregating Rs 5,000 crore by existing unitholders as the trust transitions from a privately listed InvIT to a publicly listed InvIT. Bitcoin traded near $66,300 on Wednesday, gaining about 1.7% over the past 24 hours.The recovery was supported by improving institutional demand and firmer global equity markets, extending the rebound from June’s lows. Immediate support lies around $65,400-$65,500, followed by the stronger $64,500-$65,000 region. Resistance is visible near $66,900-$67,000, with the short-term-holder breakeven area around $68,000 presenting a more significant hurdle before $70,000. On-chain conditions are becoming more balanced, although the rally is not yet being driven by strong spot demand. The spot volumes remain subdued, while futures and options open interest has expanded. Perpetual-market buying has improved, and demand for downside protection has eased. However, the growing share of short-term, price-sensitive capital leaves Bitcoin vulnerable to sharper volatility. The July 28-29 Federal Reserve meeting remains the principal macro catalyst. All 104 economists surveyed by Reuters expect rates to remain at 3.50%-3.75%, although markets see one increase by December as probable. Brent crude near $91.55 and the 10-year Treasury yield around 4.63% could keep the Fed cautious. Our advice: Investors should avoid chasing Bitcoin near resistance. Staggered accumulation and disciplined position sizing remain preferable until the price clears $68,000 with sustained spot volume and ETF demand. Gabriel India (part of the ANAND Group) is set to acquire a 29% stake in HL Mando Anand India Pvt Ltd (a joint venture with Korea’s HL Mando) from the group’s holding company (Asia Investments Pvt Ltd / related entities) as part of a larger Composite Scheme of Arrangement. This move is part of a broader restructuring aimed at consolidating diverse auto component businesses (suspensions, drivetrain, fluids, adhesives, NVH, etc.) under Gabriel India to create a diversified mobility platform. The acquisition will strengthen Gabriel’s portfolio in suspension, braking, and steering systems, primarily for passenger vehicles serving Hyundai-Kia and other OEMs. The overall scheme involves demergers and share issuances, subject to regulatory approvals, and supports the group’s growth ambitions. (TFCI) has announced its uaudited financial results for the quarter ended June 30, 2026, reporting strong growth in income and profitability while maintaining healthy asset quality and a robust capital position. TFCI’s total income increased by 75% year-on-year to ₹115.15 crore during Q1 FY27, compared with ₹65.82 crore in the corresponding quarter of the previous financial year. Interest income, reflecting the Company’s core financing operations, grew by 27% to ₹81.02 crore from ₹63.71 crore in Q1 FY26. Profit Before Tax more than doubled to ₹79.52 crore during the quarter, as against ₹38.16 crore in the corresponding period last year. Profit After Tax rose by approximately 100% to ₹61.21 crore, compared with ₹30.56 crore in Q1 FY26. TFCI continued to maintain sound asset-quality indicators. Gross NPA stood at 0.41% as of June 30, 2026, while Net NPA remained NIL. The Company maintained a provision coverage ratio of 100%, underlining its prudent provisioning and risk-management approach. The Capital Risk Adequacy Ratio remained strong at 57.13%, providing adequate headroom to support future business growth. Title: MSFT Court Alert: Microsoft Investors Seeking to Recover Losses in Securities Fraud Class Action are Notified to Contact BFA Law before August 11 Deadline
URL: https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/07/22/3331195/0/en/MSFT-Court-Alert-Microsoft-Investors-Seeking-to-Recover-Losses-in-Securities-Fraud-Class-Action-are-Notified-to-Contact-BFA-Law-before-August-11-Deadline.html
Time Published: 2026-07-22T10:47:00Z
Full Content:
July 22, 2026 06:47 ET | Source: Bleichmar Fonti & Auld Bleichmar Fonti & Auld NEW YORK, July 22, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Leading securities law firm Bleichmar Fonti & Auld LLP announces that a class action lawsuit has been filed against Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ:MSFT) and certain of the Company’s senior executives for securities fraud after its significant stock drop resulting from potential violations of the federal securities laws. If you invested in Microsoft, you are encouraged to obtain additional information by visiting: https://www.bfalaw.com/cases/microsoft-class-action-lawsuit. Key Details of the Microsoft ($MSFT) Class Action: Investors have until August 11, 2026 to ask the Court to be appointed to lead the case. The complaint asserts securities fraud claims under Sections 10(b) and 20(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 on behalf of investors in Microsoft common stock. The class action is pending in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington. It is captioned City of St. Clair Shores Police and Fire Retirement System, et al., No. 26-cv-02071. Why is Microsoft Being Sued for Securities Fraud? Microsoft is a multinational technology company that develops software, cloud services, and devices. In recent years, Microsoft’s cloud computing platform named Azure has been Microsoft’s main growth driver. A key reason for Azure’s recent growth is Microsoft’s multi-billion-dollar investment into AI, including the development of its own generative AI chatbot named Copilot. According to the complaint, during the relevant period, Microsoft consistently touted Copilot’s best-in-class capabilities, which purportedly drove widespread and growing user adoption. Copilot’s apparent success allowed Microsoft to report surging Azure-related revenue. As alleged, in truth, Copilot suffered from severe functionality issues that caused user adoption to decline and put Microsoft’s Azure revenue at risk. Why did Microsoft’s Stock Drop? On January 28, 2026, Microsoft announced disappointing 2Q 2026 financial results and that Azure growth had slowed suddenly. Microsoft also allegedly revealed for the first time that the number of Microsoft 365 Copilot premium customers totaled only 15 million, materially below analyst estimates. This news caused the price of Microsoft common stock to decline $48.13 per share, or 10%, from $481.63 per share on January 28, 2026, to $433.50 per share on January 29, 2026. Additionally, on February 3, 2026, The Wall Street Journal reported in an article titled “Microsoft’s Pivotal AI Product Is Running Into Big Problems” that severe challenges and functionality issues had plagued Copilot, causing the application to lose market share. Specifically, The Wall Street Journal reported that “[c]onfusing brand positioning and interoperability problems have frustrated users.” Click here for more information: https://www.bfalaw.com/cases/microsoft-class-action-lawsuit. What Can You Do? If you invested in Microsoft, you may have legal options and are encouraged to submit your information to the firm. All representation is on a contingency fee basis; there is no cost to you. Shareholders are not responsible for any court costs or expenses of litigation. The firm will seek court approval for any potential fees and expenses. BFA is a leading international law firm representing plaintiffs in securities class actions and shareholder litigation. It has been named a top plaintiff law firm by Chambers USA, The Legal 500, and ISS SCAS, and its attorneys have been named “Elite Trial Lawyers” by the National Law Journal, “Litigation Stars” by Benchmark Litigation, among the top “500 Leading Plaintiff Financial Lawyers” by Lawdragon, “Titans of the Plaintiffs’ Bar” by Law360 and “SuperLawyers” by Thomson Reuters. Most recently, The Legal 500 awarded BFA the most client satisfaction accolades of any plaintiff’s securities litigation law firm, with clients noting: “[t]here is no better service provider in the practice area,” “[t]he interest of the client is always front and center,” and “[t]here isn’t a better firm in this space.” One testimonial described the firm as “nimble and entrepreneurial,” with a “relentless focus on adding value for clients.” Among its recent notable successes, BFA recovered over $900 million in value from Tesla, Inc.’s Board of Directors, as well as $420 million from Teva Pharmaceutical Ind. Title: Managers Are Not Overhead: They Are Infrastructure
URL: https://www.oreilly.com/radar/managers-are-not-overhead-they-are-infrastructure/#BlogPosting
Time Published: 2026-07-22T10:42:49Z
Full Content:
With the O’Reilly learning platform, you get the resources and guidance to keep your skills sharp and stay ahead. Try it free for up to 14 days. Join a live online event on the O’Reilly platform to learn from the experts shaping tech. Please read our privacy policy. Radar > Topics > Executive Briefing What recent data reveals about the role of managers in the age of flattening orgs. By David Michelson July 22, 2026 • 5 minute read Managers have been disproportionate casualties of the rolling waves of post-COVID-19 tech layoffs that started in late 2022. Popularized by large companies such as Meta, Google, and Amazon, phrases like “flattening the org” and “reducing bureaucracy” are now synonymous with thinning the management layers that ballooned during the 2021–2022 hiring sprees. Retrospectively, such flattening can seem prescient given that AI models can now automate schedules, draft performance reviews, coordinate communication across teams, and aid in the prioritization and decision support typical of management. Pushed to the experimental extreme, this can now mean 50 ICs reporting into one supervisor. The logic here is simple and stark: Since AI can, or will soon be able to, handle a lot of what managers used to do, fewer managers are necessary. Instead, decision-making can be distributed within teams as individual contributors become more adept at orchestrating and supervising agentic workflows with increasingly refined judgment and decreased reliance on managerial oversight. Everyone, in effect, is a manager now. The problem with this narrative is that organizations are reducing managers at precisely the time they are becoming increasingly important to realizing their AI investments. Several sources of recent data back this up. A main conclusion from Microsoft’s 2026 Work Trend Index Annual Report is that “organizational factors—culture, manager support, talent practices—account for twice the reported AI impact of individual effort alone.” Once leadership sets AI strategy and incentives, “it’s managers who operationalize it, and the data shows the impact of their ability to do so.” Specifically, when managers actively modeled AI use, employees reported a 17-point lift in reported AI value, a 22-point lift in critical thinking about their AI use, and a 30-point lift in trust in agentic AI. When managers created psychological safety around experimentation, employees reported up to 20 points higher AI readiness and value—and were 1.4x more likely to be high-frequency users of agentic AI. The impact of managers is even greater on more advanced AI users, what Microsoft calls “Frontier Professionals” (16% of those surveyed, users who “use agents for multistep workflows and building multi-agent systems”). This group is more likely to report that their manager uses AI (85% vs. 64%), establishes quality standards for AI work (83% vs. 57%), encourages experimentation (84% vs. 61%), and rewards work redesign regardless of outcome (26% vs. 11%). The report notes that “in many cases, employees are moving faster than the organization around them.” Microsoft calls this the “Transformation Paradox.” According to the Microsoft data, managers are the layer that helps resolve it. They translate organizational strategy into team practices that let individual work with AI produce value. Of course, once AI adoption is the norm and managers no longer need to manage that change, one could argue that many aspects of the role remain susceptible to automation and the role will contract. We don’t know how this will play out yet, but if management roles were already contracting we would expect to see early signs, and the data shows the opposite. LeadDev’s Engineering Leadership Report 2026 surveyed 600 engineering leaders, 55% of whom are engineering managers or managers of managers. The report notes that “AI is simultaneously expanding what leaders can do technically and what is expected of them organizationally, without reducing the demands on their time in either dimension.” Not only are managers becoming more hands-on technically, but One way to interpret these figures is to say that more teams and more reports show flattening working as planned from a business perspective. Another reading—not mutually exclusive—is that the role is in transition and most organizations have not fully wrestled with what that involves: managers doing their old work at greater scale, and the new work of making AI a core team practice. Either way, that’s not contraction. Contraction would mean the scope of the role itself is shrinking as AI and ICs absorb more of the work. More teams and more reports is what flattening produces, not evidence the role is going away. To be clear, none of this means organizations should stop scrutinizing reporting structures and removing genuinely unhelpful layers of bureaucracy that stifle decision-making. But it does mean asking a harder question before the next round of cuts: Are you reducing management based on what managers used to do or based on the critical work they are doing now or will need to do next? The “what they used to do” answer treats managers like overhead. The emerging evidence suggests that managers are currently playing the role of infrastructure, the critical layer that translates AI investment into actual value at the team level. Flattening on the assumption that AI will facilitate its own adoption or that value will emerge from unguided individual effort is making a productivity bet that the data doesn’t support. Follow us Take O'Reilly with you and learn anywhere, anytime on your phone and tablet. View all O'Reilly videos, virtual conferences, and live events on your home TV. Do not sell or share my personal information . © 2026, O'Reilly Media, Inc. All trademarks and registered trademarks appearing on oreilly.com are the property of their respective owners.

Title: Kraken parent expands tokenized stocks to Hong Kong, UK and South Korea equities
URL: https://www.coindesk.com/business/2026/07/22/kraken-parent-expands-tokenized-stocks-to-hong-kong-uk-and-south-korea-equities
Time Published: 2026-07-22T10:30:00Z
Full Content:
The parent company of crypto exchange Kraken is expanding its tokenized stock offering beyond U.S. companies, betting investors want onchain access to equities from around the world. Payward, the developer of the xStocks framework, told CoinDesk it is working with investment infrastructure provider GTN to bring Hong Kong-listed stocks to the platform, with U.K., European and South Korean equities expected to follow, subject to regulatory approvals. The partnership also lays the groundwork for expanding xStocks beyond equities into other tokenized asset classes, the firm added. "The biggest asset class that hasn't been tokenized yet is the rest of the world," Mark Greenberg, global head of Payward Services, said in a statement. "One asset at a time, we're bringing truly global capital markets onchain until geography becomes irrelevant to investing." The move comes as competition in tokenized equities intensifies, with crypto firms and Wall Street institutions racing to bring stock trading onchain. Earlier this month, Robinhood (HOOD) expanded its tokenized stock offering beyond European users, and Coinbase (COIN) is also planning to offer stock tokens. The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC), the backbone of the U.S. securities markets, has begun testing tokenized securities infrastructure, while Nasdaq and the New York Stock Exchange have also started tokenization initiatives. The push points to growing conviction that tokenization — the process of representing traditional assets as blockchain-based tokens — could upgrade capital markets with faster settlement, round-the-clock trading and more efficient movement of assets. Citi estimated that tokenized securities could grow into a $5.5 trillion market by 2030, including $2.6 trillion in tokenized equities. Until now, tokenized equity platforms have largely focused on replicating U.S. markets onchain, offering blockchain-based versions of popular stocks such as Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA). Expanding xStocks to overseas markets gives investors access to a broader universe of companies, including high-flying Asian firms tied to the AI supply chain, which have become popular among global retail traders. The GTN partnership marks the next stage of xStocks, which started last year with tokenized U.S. stocks and exchange-traded funds. The platform now supports more than 500 tokenized securities, has processed more than $35 billion in trading volume and has nearly 200,000 holders, according to Payward. The products remain unavailable to U.S. investors. GTN, which connects to more than 90 global markets, will provide execution, custody and recordkeeping for the securities backing the tokens. Subject to regulatory approvals, it also plans to offer xStocks products to its institutional clients. The announcement lands as the industry debates how tokenized stocks should be issued. Projects such as xStocks rely on third-party issuers that purchase and custody traditional shares before minting tokens. Others argue securities should be issued natively on blockchain networks, eliminating intermediaries altogether. The debate is drawing increasing attention from regulators and market infrastructure providers as tokenized securities move closer to the financial mainstream. Read more: Wall Street transfer agents lobby SEC, warning that third-party tokens pose risks to market integrity Markets repositioned since June, but Binance held share (~55% user funds, ~24% spot) and drew net inflows in early July while the tracked market saw outflows. Markets repositioned since June, but Binance held share (~55% user funds, ~24% spot) and drew net inflows in early July while the tracked market saw outflows. Why it matters: Markets repositioned since June, but Binance held share (~55% user funds, ~24% spot) and drew net inflows in early July while the tracked market saw outflows. CoinDesk has adopted a set of principles aimed at ensuring the integrity, editorial independence and freedom from bias of its publications. Bullish owns and invests in digital asset businesses and digital assets and CoinDesk employees, including journalists, may receive Bullish equity-based compensation.

Title: BlackRock is raising $12bn to build Meta a data centre it will own, lend to, and rent out
URL: https://thenextweb.com/news/blackrock-12bn-meta-el-paso-data-centre-debt/
Time Published: 2026-07-22T10:23:53Z
Full Content:
The same BlackRock that just bought the world's largest data-centre developer is now raising $12bn of debt to build Meta another one. It owns the buildings, lends the money, and collects the rent. And most of the borrowing never touches Meta's balance sheet. On Monday, JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley began pitching investors on a bond sale of at least $12 billion, as The Wall Street Journal reported. The money will build a Meta data centre in El Paso, Texas. The structure is the story. BlackRock and its infrastructure and private-credit arms own 80% of the roughly one-gigawatt project. Meta, the company that will actually run AI inside it, owns just 20% and will lease the campus back. The debt sits with a BlackRock-controlled entity, not with Meta. That keeps most of the cost off Meta’s books, booked as rent rather than capital spending. It is the same off-balance-sheet trick a Nikkei study just pinned at $1.65 trillion across the five biggest US tech firms. Meta has done this before. Its Hyperion site in Louisiana used a joint venture where the credit firm Blue Owl held 80% and Meta 20%. That entity sold $27 billion of bonds last year, the largest private-debt deal on record, and BlackRock bought more than $3 billion of them. El Paso copies the blueprint. Meta also just agreed to lease a project in Shippingport, Pennsylvania, run by Aligned, the developer BlackRock bought for $40bn. The pattern is consistent: BlackRock owns the buildings, Meta rents the compute. The 💜 of EU tech The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now! For Larry Fink, the deal is a payoff. Over the past few years, the $15 trillion asset manager spent about $25 billion buying two private-market firms, Global Infrastructure Partners and HPS, as Bloomberg reported. Both now sit inside BlackRock, and both are backing El Paso. That turns an index-fund giant into an owner-operator. On this deal it does both jobs at once: it originates the asset and sells the debt against it. Fink told analysts last week that the two units were “coming together on the origination side.” The risk is the one every off-balance-sheet AI deal carries. The bonds are long-dated, but the chips inside the building depreciate in a few years, and the leases reportedly run shorter than the campuses they finance. BlackRock is betting AI demand, and Meta’s rent, hold long enough to pay it back. It also stacks much of the AI build-out inside one firm. Rivals Blue Owl and Blackstone have led the biggest data-centre financings so far, and the bubble talk is getting louder. The El Paso campus is due online in 2028. I am the Editor in Chief for TNW, covering technology not as a parade of launches and valuations, but as a system of influence, persuasion, (show all) I am the Editor in Chief for TNW, covering technology not as a parade of launches and valuations, but as a system of influence, persuasion, and change. I write about startups, venture capital, digital policy, and Europe ecosystem, with an eye on the larger story beneath them: who gets to build the future, who profits from it, and how Europe is learning to speak in a louder voice of its own. Before moving into senior editorial leadership, I've built my career for over +10 years across journalism, storytelling, content strategy, SEO, and digital publishing, with experience in SaaS, hospitality, art, and culture. Title: “If You Own Too Much Tech, You’re Going To Be Slaughtered.” Cramer Urges Investors To Take Profits
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/07/22/if-you-own-too-much-tech-youre-going-to-be-slaughtered-cramer-urges-investors-to-take-profits/
Time Published: 2026-07-22T10:22:12Z
Full Content:
Investing Cramer urges trimming Micron (MU), up 758% in a year, and rotating into JPMorgan (JPM) at just 15 times earnings. Wells Fargo (WFC) at 12 times earnings remains a laggard buy, and Novo Nordisk's (NVO) weakness makes Eli Lilly more attractive. Act now: the analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks — and Eli Lilly didn't make the cut. Grab the names FREE today. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Jimcramerphoto (CC BY 2.0) by Tulane Public Relations Jim Cramer, host of Mad Money, used his Tuesday morning appearance on CNBC’s “Squawk on the Street” on July 21, 2026 to fire a warning shot at investors who have ridden the AI trade to fresh highs and never taken a chip off the table. His message was blunt: “Because if you own too much tech, you’re going to be slaughtered. And you won’t even know what hit you. For the moment, it’s time to go to other sectors that can make you money without the volatility.” With futures pointing higher on renewed semiconductor strength, Cramer wants investors to lean against the crowd and rotate proceeds into groups that have lagged the AI melt-up. The playbook: financials and healthcare, where valuations are cleaner and earnings power is showing up in results from banks that just reported. Cramer’s rotation call is rooted in position sizing, not a top call on tech. “When you have a group that is red hot, you take off half. You have to be disciplined, and the reason why you have to be disciplined is because a lot of this last run, the parabolic move, is not cured by a 20% to 30% decline because the stock went up more than that.” Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU | MU Price Prediction) is Exhibit A. Cramer suggested a few weeks ago that investors sell half of Micron, and the numbers explain why. Shares are up 240.36% year to date and 758.78% over the past year. Fiscal Q3 2026 revenue landed at $41.456 billion, a 17.60% beat, with non-GAAP EPS of $25.11 versus $20.28 expected and GAAP gross margin expanding to 84.6% from 37.7% a year earlier. The fundamentals are real; the move is parabolic. Polymarket contracts currently price a 0.74 probability that Micron closes lower on July 22. The sector composition is already tilting. Among the top ten DOW names year to date, eight are either healthcare or financials. The July 14 bank earnings gave the rotation fresh fuel. JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) posted Q2 EPS of $7.70 versus $5.80 expected on $57.35 billion in revenue, and authorized a fresh $50 billion buyback. Cramer’s take on Jamie Dimon: “You can buy his stock for 15 times earnings. It’s one of the brightest guys in the world. Jamie and his team is filled with brilliant people. 15 times. I’ll take it.” Shares are up 8.65% YTD. Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) trades at 14 times earnings, delivered a fifth consecutive EPS beat at $1.21, and is up 13.34% YTD. Wells Fargo trades at 12 times earnings and remains -4.84% YTD, the kind of laggard Cramer is willing to buy while others chase memory chips. Cramer’s second lane is pharma, and he wants the winner of the GLP-1 war. “I will say, you know what? Let me go and buy some Lilly into the Novo Nordisk weakness.” Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) reported Q1 EPS of $8.55 versus $6.79 expected and revenue of $19.80 billion, up 55.5% YoY, then raised 2026 guidance to $82.0-$85.0 billion. Mounjaro alone did $8.66 billion, up 125%. Novo Nordisk, by contrast, is down 20.46% over the past year and guided full-year adjusted sales to a decline of 4% to 12% at constant currency. Cramer’s message is about discipline. He wants investors to recognize when a move has run past what any normal pullback could fix, trim into strength, and redeploy where earnings are compounding at reasonable multiples. Banks reporting record quarters at 12 to 15 times earnings and a pharma leader raising guidance mid-year give him a place to put the profits. The discipline is the point. Contact [email protected] for any questions or corrections. My day job is helping enterprise executives make smarter decisions about retention, compensation, and growth. These are the same operational levers that show up in every earnings report investors actually read. That perspective shapes my writing for 24/7 Wall St. The headline numbers are easy. The interesting stuff is underneath: how companies make money, what executives are worried about, and what any of it means for the person checking their 401(k) on a Sunday afternoon. I write about personal finance and business as someone who has spent her career inside the rooms where these decisions get made. Our top personal finance-related articles today. Title: “If You Own Too Much Tech, You’re Going To Be Slaughtered.” Cramer Urges Investors To Take Profits

Title: A violent AI unwind is hiding beneath a resilient stock market: Chart of the Day
URL: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/article/a-violent-ai-unwind-is-hiding-beneath-a-resilient-stock-market-chart-of-the-day-100000350.html
Time Published: 2026-07-22T10:00:00Z

Title: Tesla has a chance to steal back the spotlight from SpaceX. These 3 earnings storylines matter most.
URL: https://www.businessinsider.com/tesla-q2-earnings-preview-themes-questions-stock-price-investor-outlook-2026-7#article
Time Published: 2026-07-22T09:50:01Z
Full Content:
Tesla used to be the main event for earnings season. Retail investors hung on every number and word, and big price swings almost always ensued. Now, with the EV-maker set to report today, the buzz is minimal. Tesla has been replaced by SpaceX as the Elon Musk investment vehicle most favored by day traders. It's also decoupled from its fellow Magnificent 7 members — especially hyperscalers like Microsoft and Meta — as Musk largely funnels his AI ambitions into SpaceX. But just because Tesla is out of the zeitgeist for the moment doesn't mean it'll stay quiet forever. One surprise or major announcement from Musk could reinvigorate a stock that's been left out of the 2026 market rally, and has struggled mightily to reclaim record levels. As the chart below shows, shares are down 16% year to date, having reached all-time highs in December 2025. It's been a volatile grind higher for years. In Elon We Trust. Here are three main themes investors should watch heading into Tesla earnings after the closing bell today: Tesla is a fairly unique company in that its most closely watched metric — vehicle deliveries — was already reported for the quarter a few weeks ago. So investors aren't going to be hanging on a specific number. They're instead going to be watching for updates on Tesla's robotaxi business, as well as its Optimus robot. For robotaxis, investors will be watching for updates on the pace of expansion, new cities, the safety and regulatory process, and any timeline guidance on the Cybercab. For Optimus, they'll be looking for signs that the humanoid robot is moving from concept to commercial reality. Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Once a fringe idea, the prospect of Musk's two public-market titans merging has gathered steam. Ahead of earnings, shareholders took to an online Tesla investor forum to ask executives about it. Given the interest, it wouldn't be surprising if the merger came up on the call. Longtime Tesla investor Ross Gerber expects it to. If it does, what Musk or the company says could be the biggest headline from the report. Did you ever think we'd be talking about Tesla's EV business — once one of the stock market's foremost speculative moonshots — as a rock-solid source of capital for even more ambitious endeavors? Well, we're there. All across the stock market, tech firms need money for capex. They need it now, and a lot of it. There are always capital markets, which Tesla does tap, but the EV business is the engine that makes it run. The question for investors is a familiar one, just in a different industry. Do they think Tesla is overspending, and are they satisfied with how they're getting money to use? It's the type of question we'll be hearing continuously throughout earnings season. Title: Energy Security and Climate Realism Usher in Coal’s Rise
URL: https://wattsupwiththat.com/2026/07/22/energy-security-and-climate-realism-usher-in-coals-rise/
Time Published: 2026-07-22T09:00:00Z
Full Content:
The world's most viewed site on global warming and climate change By Vijay Jayaraj Much of the global energy conversation still pretends that coal is on its way out. The pretense may be comforting to activists, investors, and policymakers who built their plans for a transition to so-called clean energy that never acknowledged the limitations of solar and wind power. However, the newest work by Lars Schernikau and Portia Roberts, The Coal Reality That Western Policy Ignores, shows the opposite: Coal is not being replaced, but rather being layered on top of everything else. The data reveal that global coal consumption is accelerating. The appetite for reliable, baseload power drove usage to an unprecedented high of approximately 9 billion tons in 2025. This volume represents more coal mined and burned than at any time in history, far outstripping levels recorded before the multi-trillion-dollar Western net-zero campaign. In 2025, China alone brought on-line 80 gigawatts of new coal-fired generation – enough to power 60 million households. The Iran war’s disruption to supplies of liquefied natural gas has further boosted demand for coal as nations switched fuels for electricity production. This strategic imperative drove a massive surge in coal shipments across the Pacific. Bangladesh, Japan, the Philippines, South Korea, Taiwan, and Vietnam immediately increased coal imports to avoid blackouts, with China and India leading the shift. While alternative energy installations have expanded, coal utilization has grown right alongside them. The two asset classes are not locked in a zero-sum game but are growing to meet a voracious demand for electricity and industrial production. The world’s proven reserves of coal – deposits that can be extracted economically utilizing current technologies – possess an operational runway of 130 to 150 years at today’s consumption rates. Taking into account remaining geological reserves, Earth holds enough anthracite and bituminous coal for many hundreds of years. Schernikau and Roberts also remind readers that coal sits at the center of food security. Roughly a quarter of the world depends on fertilizers made with coal-derived ammonia. Abandon coal too quickly and you risk not only blackouts but also food production and, ultimately, lives. This return to dependable solid fuels coincides with a long-overdue collapse of the pseudoscientific architecture that sustained for decades the narrative of a fabricated climate crisis. For years, scenarios of extreme weather based on flawed climate models were systematically weaponized by activist bureaucrats to demand the dismantling of a hydrocarbon economy. The most prominent of these fabrications was Representative Concentration Pathway 8.5 (RCP 8.5), alongside its companion RCP 7.0. The international modeling consensus has formally abandoned these deceptions, confirming that RCP 8.5 is officially “implausible.” The apocalyptic scenarios used to terrorize the public and coerce financial markets were never rooted in real-world data. The institutional framework designed to starve conventional energy sectors of investment capital is unraveling before our eyes. The corporate campaign for Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) compliance has had a severe global retrenchment. Global investment consortia, led by institutional giants like BlackRock, have quietly dismantled or significantly downgraded strict climate mandates. International banking groups and global aviation associations have canceled or rolled back aggressive net-zero timelines. The fantasy of a rapid, painless transition away from hydrocarbons has run headfirst into physical laws and geopolitical necessities. Weather-dependent power technologies cannot run steel mills, operate heavy manufacturing, or secure domestic food supplies. The climate establishment can keep writing obituaries for coal, but the market keeps resurrecting it because there is no substitute for its scale, reliability, and availability. Stop pretending that every rise in renewables implies a fall in coal. Stop using extreme scenarios to frighten the public into accepting policy mandates that do not fit the needs of growing economies. And stop telling developing nations that they must sacrifice dependable power in the name of predictive models that no longer command trust. Originally published at PJ Media, July 15th, 2026. Vijay Jayaraj is a Science and Research Associate at the CO2 Coalition, Fairfax, Virginia. He holds an M.S. in environmental sciences from the University of East Anglia and a postgraduate degree in energy management from Robert Gordon University, both in the U.K., and a bachelor’s in engineering from Anna University, India. He served as a research associate with the Changing Oceans Research Unit at University of British Columbia, Canada. Tags: China, coal, ESG, India, Vijay Jayaraj I still remember the dramatic photos of great clouds of “smoke” belching from the “smokestacks” of coal plants. It was all a big lie, and part of the War on Coal. What we were seeing was actually water vapor, but people didn’t know that, or didn’t care. The effect was based on emotion, not logic, and that was by design. And it was always back lit photos of colling towers that were used to show how polluting CO2 is. I took this up with the BBC and the response was they were just using stock photos and had no control over the lighting Dumb excuse, they certainly could have had one of their “journalists” drive to a site and take an honest photo. I bet there were more accurate stock photos available too but of course they’d pick the one that looks the most hellish. yep I remember once being shown a dark cloud coming out of a cooling tower by a Greenpiss climate twerp.. I had to explain to him that it was actually a nuclear power plant cooling tower.. .. he still didn’t understand. ! It’s funny you should mention this. I took a tour of a very modern coal-fired plant couple of years ago, and the plant manager told me that they don’t schedule tours if at all possible when they know that water vapor will be highly visible condensing out the stack. Pereception is sometimes far more meaningful than a truthful explanation. In my various training courses (mandatory) it is always emphasized that perception is more important than reality. — “It doesn’t matter what is true, it only matters what people believe is true.” – Paul Watson, co-founder of Greenpeace. There is a simple solution to visible condensate from a stack. Use waste heat to reduce relative humidity at the top of the stack to about 90%. The result is a plume that forms like a cloud separated from the stack. It turns something that some regard as ugly into a serenely calming sight – fluffy, almost white cloud some distance from the stack. Cooling towers not so easy but they are not as high or as visible. It was our response to the so-called energy crisis of the 1960s to 1980 or thereabouts. We thought the age of coal was over, with petroleum liquids taking over things like even electricity generation, but then disruptions to global petroleum markets made us realize the enormous coal resources of the U.S. and that these were a low cost resource. I remember in 2005/2006 here in Texas, Governor Rick Perry was about to help usher in 11 (eleven) new coal power plants. Only wealthier countries are pursuing favorable weather conditions to support electricity generation by wind turbines and solar panels. Shockingly, Pew Research Center shows about 70% of the 8 billion on this planet, or almost 6 billion, are living on less than $10/day and have yet to join the industrial revolution! For those is poorer developing countries, using abundant coal for the generation of continuous and uninterruptable electricity is best for their country’s’ economic development. “The world’s proven reserves of coal – deposits that can be extracted economically utilizing current technologies – possess an operational runway of 130 to 150 years at today’s consumption rates.” This is the issue that is often overlooked by the AGW skeptics. Whilst I am also an AGW skeptic, I try to look at the broader picture of future energy use. As Ronald Stein mentioned in his post above, “Shockingly, Pew Research Center shows about 70% of the 8 billion on this planet, or almost 6 billion, are living on less than $10/day and have yet to join the industrial revolution!” If one is concerned about the future prosperity and wellbeing of the world population, then we should plan for an energy consumption which is far, far greater than today’s consumption rate. Relying only on fossil fuels would result in a future catastrophe resulting from energy costs which would be much higher than today’s costs, because of the increasing scarity of fossil fuels in far less than 150 years, assuming world poverty is addressed. This is why we should begin the development of alternative and renewable energy sources right now, because it is clear that such development is a long process, involving lots of inefficiences and hazards that have to be addressed. I consider the current ‘net zero’ policy of mandates and subsidies merely a stimulant to encourage research and experimentation into alternative sources of energy. What doesn’t work efficiently will (or at least should) eventually be discarded. You are right that any energy transition is going to take a long time-many decades and include all forms of energy generation that should compete naturally without subsidies. Presently, coal at scale with modern HELE plants is the cheapest generator of base load electricity, gas is great for backup as is hydro, whilst nuclear is gaining ground as a safe reliable long-term producer. Wind and solar are increasingly efficient, but are less productive and unreliable than fossil fuels, and need critical backup, that can’t presently be provided using batteries for more than a few hours- certainly not days!. Essentially, we need better technology and further costly research if we want to replace fossil fuels. Thus, Net Zero is a loser from all reasonable perspectives and need to be cancelled ASAP before it de-industrialises economies and prevents the ability of nations to undertake required energy research and have the industrial capacity to make new technology. “Wind and solar are increasingly efficient, but are less productive and unreliable than fossil fuels, and need critical backup, that can’t presently be provided using batteries for more than a few hours- certainly not days!.” I’m not in favour of using large tracts of land for windmills and solar panels, and the additional requirement for long-distance power lines. However, the roofs of buildings are essentially free spaces. Imagine a new home designed to maximize the the production of solar energy by integrating the construction of the house with the installation of solar tiles or solar panels which cover the entire roof area, apart from the solar hot water heater. An average all-electric Australian home, with a solar hot water heater, consumes between 11 and 15 kWh of electricity per day. 60KWh of sodium-ion battery storage would provide 4-5 full days of power supply for the home, when the sun doesn’t shine. A bit more than a few hours! Notice how Vijay’s posts get ever more assertive as we “deniers” take the “wind ” out of the thermometer-readers’ “sales”. (Sorry, couldn’t resist.) Title: NVIDIA Agent Toolkit が新たな Omniverse ライブラリで拡張され、AI エージェントによるシミュレーション対応のワールド構築が可能に
URL: https://prtimes.jp/main/html/rd/p/000000637.000012662.html
Time Published: 2026-07-22T07:40:49Z
Full Content:
PR TIMESのご利用について 資料をダウンロード NVIDIA ニュース概要： NVIDIA Agent Toolkit に NVIDIA Omniverse ライブラリが新たに追加され、AI エージェントは、ソフトウェア開発者が既存のアプリケーションにフィジカル AI 機能を統合できるよう支援 NVIDIA RTX センサー シミュレーション、GPU アクセラレーションによる物理シミュレーション、およびシミュレーション対応アセットの検証を行うための新しい Omniverse ライブラリが、GitHub で公開 SideFX と PTC は、フィジカル AI 向けの 3D アプリケーションに Omniverse ライブラリを統合。これらは、NVIDIA RTX Spark から NVIDIA DGX Station に至るまで、クラウドおよびローカルの AI システムをサポート Blender に Omniverse ライブラリを統合するための新しい NVIDIA ブループリントが公開 NVIDIA は本日、NVIDIA Agent Toolkit に NVIDIA Omniverse™ ライブラリが組み込まれたことを発表しました。これは、既存のアプリケーションにフィジカル AI 機能を付加し、シミュレーション用の 3D コンテンツを準備するために必要なツールやスキルを AI エージェントに提供する、ソフトウェア コンポーネントのコレクションです。 ロボットや工場、自律型システムは、現実世界で稼働する前に、シミュレーション環境で設計、テスト、トレーニングを行う必要があります。シミュレーション用の 3D コンテンツを準備するには、単に見た目がリアルであるだけでは不十分です。アセットには、適切な構造、マテリアル、スケール、ラベル、センサー、そして物理特性が備わっている必要があります。NVIDIA Agent Toolkit に NVIDIA Omniverse ライブラリが統合されたことで、AI エージェントはワークフローの構築、シーンの検証、問題の指摘、アセットの準備を行うためのツールやスキルを備えることになります。これにより、開発者は 3D コンテンツからシミュレーション可能な環境への移行をより迅速に進められるようになります。 NVIDIA の創業者/CEO であるジェンスン フアン (Jensen Huang) は次のように述べています。「フィジカル AI の時代は、まずシミュレーションの中で築かれるでしょう。Omniverse ライブラリを組み込んだ NVIDIA Agent Toolkit は、開発者がすでに使用している 3D ツールに AI エージェントを導入します。これにより、ロボットや工場、自律型システムが現実世界に投入されるはるか前に、それらをトレーニングしテストするための、シミュレーション対応の環境構築が可能になるのです」 SideFX や PTC といったソフトウェア メーカーは、エージェント対応のセンサー シミュレーション、物理演算、アセット検証を実現するために Omniverse ライブラリを統合しています。これにより、開発者やテクニカル アーティストが 3D コンテンツ制作ですでに使用しているアプリケーションやワークフローに、自律的に動作するエージェント型 AI を組み込むことが可能になります。 Omniverse ライブラリが NVIDIA Agent Toolkit にフィジカル AI の機能を提供 NVIDIA Agent Toolkit は、ツール、スキル、データソースを連携させる AI エージェントの構築を行うソフトウェア メーカーを支援します。Omniverse ライブラリは、既存のアプリケーション内で呼び出し可能なセンサー シミュレーション、GPU アクセラレーションによる物理演算、シミュレーション対応アセットの検証といった機能を提供し、それらのエージェントの能力を 3D およびフィジカル AI のワークフローへと拡張します。 ovrtx、ovphysx、CAD-to-SimReady スキルなど、新しい Omniverse ライブラリが GitHub で公開されました。これにより、AI エージェントは、シーンの検証、変更のテスト、シミュレーション用 3D アセットの準備といったワークフローを構築するためのツールを利用できるようになります。また、Blender に Omniverse ライブラリを統合するための新しいブループリントも GitHub で利用可能になりました。 これらのライブラリの主な機能は以下の通りです。 NVIDIA RTX センサー シミュレーション: ovrtx は、アプリケーションが 3D シーンからカメラ、LiDRA、レーダー、その他のセンサー出力を生成するのに役立ちます。そのため、開発者と AI エージェントは、フィジカル AI システムが仮想環境をどのように認識するかをテストできます。 物理的動作: ovphysx は、GPU で高速化された物理学を使用して、衝突、質量、摩擦、運動などの特性を用いて 3D シーンに現実的な挙動をもたらすため、チームは最初にオブジェクトとシステムがシミュレーションでどのように相互作用するかをテストできます。 シミュレーション対応 3D オブジェクト: CAD-to-SimReady スキルは、コンピューター支援設計 (CAD) データを OpenUSD 上に構築された SimReady アセットに変換するのに役立ち、フィジカルAI シミュレーションと仮想テストに必要な特性を 3D コンテンツに与えます。 ソフトウェア開発企業が Omniverse ライブラリを活用した開発に着手 SideFX や PTC といったソフトウェア メーカーに加え、ForgeCAD、Lightwheel、Moonlake AI、Palatial などのスタートアップ企業が NVIDIA Agent Toolkit の一部となった Omniverse ライブラリをいち早く採用し、開発に活用し始めています。 SideFX は、OpenUSD ワークフローや ovrtx、ovphysx ライブラリを使用し、エージェントが同社の「Houdini」によるプロシージャル 3D コンテンツ制作ワークフローに Omniverse ライブラリを統合する上でどのような役割を果たせるかを模索しています。これにより、テクニカル アーティストは、コンテンツの生成、物理挙動のテスト、そしてシミュレーションに向けた準備を行うための新たな手段を得ることになります。 SideFX の社長兼 CEO である Kim Davidson 氏は次のように話しています。「シミュレーション、ロボティクス、産業用 AI に求められる、複雑かつ制御可能な世界を構築するには、プロシージャル 3D 制作が不可欠です。NVIDIA Omniverse ライブラリと OpenUSD を活用することで、SideFX はエージェント対応ツールが Houdini のワークフローをどのように支援できるかを検証しています。これにより、テクニカル アーティストはクリエイティブなプロセスを自らコントロールしつつ、シミュレーション用のプロシージャル コンテンツのレビュー、テスト、準備を行えるようになります」 PTC Onshape CAD および製品データ管理 (PDM) プラットフォームは、OpenUSD と ovrtx を活用して、クラウドネイティブな設計ワークフローと物理シミュレーションを連携させています。これにより、製品設計データと、CAD、PDM、コラボレーション、シミュレーションといった各ワークフローとの一貫性が保たれるよう支援しています。 PTC の社長兼 CEO である Neil Barua 氏は次のように話しています。「エンジニアリング チームは、開発プロセス全体を通じて、設計、コラボレーション、シミュレーションをより緊密に連携させる方法を求めています。PTC と NVIDIA の取り組みはこの広範なビジョンを支えるものであり、NVIDIA Omniverse ライブラリは、検証やテストを製品設計の現場により近い段階で実施可能にするシミュレーション対応ワークフローの実現に貢献しています」 SIGGRAPH で展示された「SimReady」Blender は、NVIDIA Omniverse ライブラリと NVIDIA NemoClaw™ を使用して Blender 上で構築されたサンプル ワークフローです。これは、クリエイターが制御を維持したまま、NVIDIA RTX センサー シミュレーション、物理演算、検証機能といったエージェント対応のシミュレーション機能を、既存の 3D アプリケーションにどのように組み込めるかを示すものです。現在、このワークフローは、Blender への Omniverse ライブラリ統合に向けたブループリントとして公開されています。 また、このデモでは、NVIDIA Agent Toolkit の一部である Omniverse ライブラリを使用して構築されたこれらのワークフローが、NVIDIA RTX Spark™ を搭載したコンパクトな RTX システムから、NVIDIA GB300 搭載の NVIDIA DGX Station™ に至るまで、どのようにローカル環境で実行できるかを紹介しています。RTX Spark システムは、今秋より ASUS、Dell Technologies、HP、Lenovo、Microsoft Surface、MSI から発売され、その後 Acer や GIGABYTE からもモデルが投入される予定です。DGX Station システムは、ASUS、Dell、GIGABYTE、HP、MSI、Supermicro、Exxact から注文可能です。 NVIDIA Inception プログラムに参加している企業を含むスタートアップ各社も、Omniverse のライブラリやスキルを活用し、エージェントが支援するアセットおよびシーン準備のワークフローを導入しています。Palatial は、Omniverse の CAD-to-SimReady スキルを使用し、CAD データから SimReady アセットの作成と検証を大規模に自動化しています。Lightwheel は、OpenUSD を基盤とする Omniverse Content Agents を同社の SimReadyGen 技術に活用し、テキスト プロンプトから物理的に正確な SimReady アセットを生成しています。 ForgeCAD や Moonlake AI は、Omniverse の機能を活用したエージェント駆動型の 3D コンテンツ ワークフローを模索しており、フィジカル AI シミュレーション用のアセット生成、拡張、準備の効率化に取り組んでいます。 SIGGRAPH での NVIDIA 基調講演をぜひご覧ください。NVIDIA Omniverse ライブラリの詳細や、利用可能なサンプルおよびドキュメントについてもご確認ください。 ※本発表資料は米国時間 2026 年 7 月 20 日に発表されたプレスリリースの抄訳です。 NVIDIA について NVIDIA (NASDAQ: NVDA) はAIとアクセラレーテッド コンピューティングの世界的なリーダーです。 Title: Book Review: The Toll Of Fortune
URL: https://www.americanthinker.com/articles/2026/07/book-review-the-toll-of-fortune/
Time Published: 2026-07-22T04:00:00Z
Full Content:
A literary masterpiece that mirrors much of society today. Twilight Patriot | July 22, 2026 For a little more than a year, I have been in the habit of reviewing works of fiction by right-leaning authors who are sympathetic to the worldview of Twilight Patriot. It was my friend Alexander Macris, from Contemplations on the Tree of Woe, who helped me get this started: Alex has always admired right-wingers who push back on left-wing cultural dominance by creating art of their own. Today’s entry is The Toll of Fortune: An Indo-European Origin Saga, by A. J. R. Klopp, who was kind enough to send me a free copy last month. The Toll of Fortune is a hard book to put into a category. Is it historical fiction? Not really. It’s set in 3309 BC, well before the invention of writing, and the cultures that appear in it are known only through archaeology and comparative linguistics. Is it fantasy? Sort of: Klopp’s characters believe in a lot of things that we would call supernatural, but they don’t live in an invented world with its own languages and races and geography — Klopp is writing about our actual ancestors. The author of The Toll of Fortune is a man of diverse interests and experiences. He studied physics and astronomy in college, then spent a few years doing radio astronomy near the summit of Mauna Kea, where he studied star formation rates in distant galaxies. Then he decided to move on to something more human, and went to law school. After three years practicing corporate law (he specialized in acquisitions, mergers, and debt financing) he ended up working as a bond trader for BlackRock. But now, he’s finally found the time for his “real passion,” which “is history, especially retracing the steps of our ancestors to the remote past.” The Toll of Fortune is an adventure story in the mould of the great epics, and the main action begins when a group of Proto-Indo-European youths (the Danger-Runner band, away from home on the rite-of-passage that will make them into men) are massacred by primeval monsters long thought to be extinct. (Neanderthals, perhaps? Here is Alexander Macris’s article about the possibility that Homo neanderthalensis used to eat H. sapiens.) After a few survivors limp back to the Great Tent, bearing horrid wounds, the Clan-Father Ever-Wheel gathers a “special warband, whose purpose will be to avenge the Danger-Runner band and end the race of whichever miscreants committed this heinous act!” Thus begins our adventure – but it’s not just a battle between the heroes and the fallen elder race that they sometimes call “the Titans” or “the Gods’ First Born Children.” This warband will also encounter a strange society ruled from the Broad-Eave Hall, a literal longhouse whose Kin-Lord and Maven preside over a way of life very different from their own. And it is through the foil of the Maven’s people that the reader is able to see Ever-Wheel’s people as the heroes, even though (like any group of copper-age warriors) they do a lot of things that seem barbaric to us. Barbarism is a good thing, Klopp seems to be saying, when you compare it to civilization gone wrong. The heroes of The Toll of Fortune are Proto-Indo-Europeans, the speakers of a language that’s ancestral to the mother tongues of about half the people in the world today. All of the Germanic languages (including English), plus Spanish and French and the other Romance languages, along with Greek, Russian, Iranian, and most of the languages of India, are all descended from a single language that was spoken on the Pontic Steppe, in what is now Ukraine and southern Russia, about five thousand years ago. The people who spoke it were cattle-herders, and they were probably also the first people to ever ride horses. Needless to say, this was a skill that served them well in their various wars of conquest, as they spread far and wide at the expense of less adaptive civilizations. In a way, it’s odd that most people know so little about the Proto-Indo-Europeans, since anyone with Caucasian ancestry is, in large part, their descendant. And they also passed down bits and pieces of their worldview to us. Do you pray to a deity called Father-in-Heaven? So did they. Do you eat cattle, pigs, and chickens with gusto, while recoiling from dog meat? So did they. Most ancient cultures did not have those quirks. Klopp’s thesis is that we should know and care more about our ancestors. And he tries to draw their society as accurately as he can, based on the latest discoveries in archeology and paleo genomics. The characters that he creates are strange, and yet in some ways familiar. They don’t live by Christian ethics (no one did, three millennia before Christ) but despite their raiding and reaving of the “Farm People” whom they look down upon, they are shown, over and over again, to be more honorable, and more like us, than the various enemies they have to fight. It’s the mark of a non-woke mind that one can honor his ancestors for what they did right, regardless of the big differences in custom and morality between their times and ours. And that shouldn’t stop with the American Founders, or the Pilgrims on the Mayflower, or the Crusader kings. Before any of those people, at the very headwaters of what became Western Civilization, are the Proto-Indo-Europeans. A hunter named Wolf and his son Bear are the central characters of The Toll of Fortune, and they both make it all the way to the final battle with the Titans. But Wolf isn’t quite a member of the tribe in which he lives — he married into it, and he’s always been somewhat of an outsider, someone who’s not expected to ever join the Shouting Council that advises the Clan Fathe But the other men respect Wolf for what he is – a man who’s brave, quick-thinking, skilled in combat, and full of practical knowledge about the beasts and monsters that never stop menacing mankind. When we first meet him, he’s saving a vassal tribe from being annihilated by “wolves in dogs’ skins,” vile creatures who can give men what we would call rabies, but what Wolf and his 16-year-old son Bear call the morbus bite. Again and again, we are introduced to the Indo-European worldview through conversations between fathers and sons, like this one: Bear: ‘Who is Chaos, Father?’ Wolf: ‘Chaos takes on many forms, boy. But Chaos is not a god like the Sky-Father or the Lord of Storms – Chaos was here long before the gods and will remain even after they are gone and the sky crashes down upon us, and mountains are blown to dust by the Krivach.’ Bear digested these words slowly. He’d never heard of sacrifices to any god who came before the Sky-Father, certainly not this ‘chaos’ fellow. Wolf: ‘Chaos is the yawning chasm that existed when even time itself had a dawn. Chaos is the Ever-Serpent, coiling itself about the roots of the Axle-Tree at the base of the World, impelling its growth and causing its rot. Chaos knows all and yet knows nothing. It grants no intercession on account of offerings or sacrifice. So do not try! But that does not mean Chaos will never present you with gifts, either. These gifts will manifest in ways that can only be seen by the keenest eye.’ Later on: Wolf: ‘Long before Rain and your mother’s people brought cattle to these plains, they too were hunters. The same as my own forefathers. And before them an Age of Ice held sway – some say since the beginning of time, for the Sun – that your mother’s people call the Sky-Father – had yet to appear. During that time, beasts with fangs the size of my arm preyed on our ancestors in the darkness. In those days only the Lord of Storms, called Perqueunos, ruled the sky. And only when he defeated the Great Serpent, could we flourish.’ And then there is this: Bear: ‘Why do the Farm People make slaves?’ Wolf: ‘Because they can. Because they are weak and because those they take as slaves are weaker still.’ Bear: ‘Shouldn’t we take slaves too then?’ Wolf: ‘Strong men do not need slaves. Resourceful men do not want slaves. And cunning men have no use for an enslaved mind….’ Bear: ‘But we could if we desired it?...’ It makes one think of the discourse about slavery during the American Founding, when a lot of people agreed that slavery was bad for society, but they didn’t always agree about why, and they weren’t passionate enough to actually put a stop to it. But if the Proto-Indo-Europeans were ambivalent about how to treat people weaker than themselves, there was no equivocating about the other side of the issue. They themselves must never be weak. Indeed, Klopp is really talking to us moderns when he has the old Clan-Father say: ‘These lands are not our lands. Not yet. They belonged to others who have left them to waste. But they will be your lands, my son, if you can stay here long enough... But beware. One day, someone might lurk over the horizon. Someone who waits until you are weakened, and leaving the land to waste, and then pushes you out.’ What actually happens in The Toll of Fortune? Well, a lot of the same things that you’ll see in any good adventure novel. There are battles, long journeys into strange lands, and scenes inspired by Homer, Shakespeare, and the Beowulf poet. There are great warriors and great smiths. There is a man who takes a meteorite (the only source of iron that existed back then) and forges it into the Hammer of Heaven. The Toll of Fortune is the tale of a boy becoming a man, and then becoming more than a man. And it is a tale about men who must fight the monsters of primeval chaos, and then turn around and fight a new kind of monster, the society of the Broad-Eave Hall, whose drugged-out, faux-matriarchal rulers are there to show that a civilization that’s too confident in its own knowledge, and too controlling of its people, can be worse than barbarism. In case you’re interested in reading more, I have a longer and more detailed review of The Toll of Fortune at the Twilight Patriot Substack. And if you don’t need any more convincing, then you can go straight ahead and purchase Klopp’s masterpiece here. Twilight Patriot is the pen name for a young American who lives in South Carolina, where he is currently working toward a graduate degree. Image: Book cover. Now more than ever, the ability to speak our minds is crucial to the republic we cherish. If what you see on American Thinker resonates with you, please consider supporting our work with a donation of as much or as little as you can give. Every dollar contributed helps us pay our staff and keep our ideas heard and our voices strong. Thank you.
--------------------------------------------------
